plastic.label: Plastic
plastic.co2_stat: Recycling 1 kg of plastic saves 1.5 kg of CO₂ and prevents 500 years of landfill decay.
steps: Rinse containers to remove any food residue — dirty plastic is unrecyclable.
Separate by type: check the number inside the recycling triangle (1–7).
Crush bottles to reduce volume before handing over.
Drop off at a dry waste collection point or schedule a kabadiwala pickup.
Never burn plastic — it releases toxic dioxins into the air.

food.label: Food Waste
food.co2_stat: 1 kg of food waste in landfill creates 2.5 kg of methane — 80x more potent than CO₂.
steps: Separate wet waste (food) from dry waste immediately — do not mix.
If you have a garden, start a simple compost pile or use a compost bin.
For bulk waste (café / restaurant), list it on the community board for a local farmer or composter.
Check your city's door-to-door wet waste collection schedule.
Cooked food can be donated to nearby NGOs or shelters before it spoils.

ewaste.label: E-Waste
ewaste.co2_stat: 1 discarded battery contaminates up to 600,000 litres of groundwater.
steps: Do NOT throw electronics in regular bins — heavy metals leach into soil and water.
Remove batteries separately — they require their own e-waste channel.
Check if the manufacturer offers a take-back or exchange programme.
Drop off at an authorised e-waste collection centre.
Functioning devices (old phones, laptops) can be donated to schools or NGOs.

paper.label: Paper
paper.co2_stat: Recycling 1 tonne of paper saves 17 trees and 26,000 litres of water.
steps: Keep paper dry — wet or greasy paper cannot be recycled.
Remove plastic wrapping, tape, or staples before bundling.
Bundle newspapers, cardboard, and office paper separately.
Sell or donate to a local kabadiwala or paper recycler.
Shred sensitive documents before handing over for privacy.

metal.label: Metal / Scrap
metal.co2_stat: Recycling aluminium uses 95% less energy than producing it from raw ore.
steps: Separate ferrous metals (iron, steel) from non-ferrous (aluminium, copper, brass).
Clean off any food or liquid residue from tins and cans.
Flatten large metal items to reduce storage space.
Contact a scrap metal dealer — most will offer a per-kg rate and pickup.
For small quantities, aggregate with a neighbour and call together for better pricing.

hazardous.label: Hazardous
hazardous.co2_stat: One litre of used motor oil can contaminate 1 million litres of drinking water.
steps: Store hazardous waste (paint, chemicals, oil, pesticides) in their original sealed containers.
Do NOT pour down the drain, sink, or toilet.
Keep away from children, pets, and flammable materials.
Find the nearest authorised hazardous waste facility from the map below.
Contact your municipal corporation for scheduled hazardous waste collection drives.

textile.label: Textiles
textile.co2_stat: Extending the life of a garment by 9 months reduces its carbon, water, and waste footprint by 20–30%.
steps: Sort into wearable (donate) and non-wearable (repurpose or recycle).
Wash wearable clothes before donating — it matters to recipients.
Drop off at an NGO collection point, old-age home, or orphanage.
Non-wearable fabric can be used as cleaning rags or stuffing material.
Some brands (H&M, Zara, Decathlon) run in-store textile collection programmes.

other.label: Other
other.co2_stat: General waste in landfills generates up to 1.5 kg of CO₂ equivalent per kg over its lifetime.
steps: Identify the dominant material in the waste and treat it accordingly.
If it contains multiple materials, disassemble and separate where possible.
Check if any part of it has a resale or reuse value before discarding.
For bulk or bulky waste, contact your municipal solid waste department.
When in doubt, do not mix with food or recyclables — keep general waste separate.
